name: data-fetching
context: Data fetching patterns in Next.js — Server Components, fetch API, caching, revalidation, streaming, and client-side fetching with SWR
info: Next.js provides multiple data fetching strategies across both routers. In the App Router, Server Components can fetch data directly with async/await using the native `fetch` API or any async data source (databases, ORMs, APIs). Fetch requests are automatically deduplicated and can be cached with time-based or on-demand revalidation. Streaming with Suspense enables progressive rendering of data-dependent UI. Client Components use hooks like SWR or React Query for browser-side data fetching. In the Pages Router, `getStaticProps` fetches at build time (with optional ISR), `getServerSideProps` fetches per request, and `getStaticPaths` defines dynamic routes for static generation. Understanding when to use each pattern is critical for building performant Next.js applications.
triggers: how to load data in server components
when to use getStaticProps vs getServerSideProps

sections.title: Server Component data fetching
sections.body: Server Components in the App Router can fetch data directly with async/await. Use `fetch()` for HTTP APIs, or call databases, ORMs, and internal services directly. Server Component code never ships to the client, so secrets and credentials are safe. Fetch requests are automatically deduplicated within a single render — multiple components fetching the same URL only make one request.

sections.title: Caching and revalidation
sections.body: Next.js extends `fetch()` with caching: `cache: 'force-cache'` (default) caches indefinitely, `cache: 'no-store'` disables caching, `next: { revalidate: N }` enables time-based revalidation every N seconds, `next: { tags: ['name'] }` enables on-demand revalidation via `revalidateTag()`. Route-level `export const revalidate = N` sets default revalidation for all fetches in a route segment. Use `revalidatePath()` and `revalidateTag()` in Server Actions for on-demand invalidation.

sections.title: Streaming with Suspense
sections.body: Wrap async Server Components in `<Suspense fallback={<Loading />}>` to stream them progressively. The shell renders instantly with the fallback, then the completed component HTML is streamed when data is ready. Use `loading.tsx` for route-level Suspense or wrap individual components for granular control. Streaming reduces Time to First Byte (TTFB) and enables fast initial page loads even with slow data sources.

sections.title: Client-side data fetching
sections.body: Client Components use hooks for browser-side fetching: `useSWR` (Vercel's SWR library) provides caching, revalidation, and error handling. React Query (`@tanstack/react-query`) offers similar features with more control. The React `use()` API can consume promises passed from Server Components. Client-side fetching is best for user-specific data, real-time updates, and interactive features that need frequent refetching.

sections.title: Fetching patterns
sections.body: **Parallel**: Start multiple fetches simultaneously with `Promise.all()` to avoid waterfalls. **Sequential**: Await fetches in order when results depend on each other (e.g., user → user's posts). **Preloading**: Call `preload()` functions early to start data fetching before components that need it render. **Deduplication**: React and Next.js automatically deduplicate identical `fetch()` calls within a single render pass.
